conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me start by asking you some questions to assess your eligibility.

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is over 16, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you! Since Alex is in approved education, that's good for your eligibility.

Now I need to ask: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does. Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for that information.

Just a few more questions: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: **Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't. Alex doesn't work 24 hours a week, and he's not receiving any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me evaluate your situation:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided:
- Alex is 17 years old and studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education
- He lives with you in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- He's not working 24+ hours per week or receiving benefits in his own right

You meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. You can continue to receive Child Benefit for Alex as long as he remains in approved education or training and is under 20 years old.

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, which you would need to pay back through Self Assessment.

If Alex's circumstances change (such as leaving education, starting work 24+ hours per week, or starting to receive benefits), you should inform HMRC as this could affect your entitlement.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is in approved education (studying for A-levels at sixth form college). According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives with you in the UK, which satisfies the requirement that you are responsible for a child and live in the UK. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, which means there is no conflict of claims. Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week and is not receiving benefits in his own right, which means he has not triggered any of the circumstances that would stop Child Benefit. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for claiming Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with their 17-year-old son Alex, who is studying for A-levels at sixth form college. Since Alex is in approved education, under 20 years old, lives with the claimant, is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in his own right, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit.
